raw.notes: Georgian royal house; Russian Imperial counts.

raw.regions_all: Tebriz; Kazvin; İsfahan; Azerbaycan; Horasan; Irak (geçici); Gürcistan; Herat (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Safavids were the dynasty that transformed Iran into a Twelver Shī'ī state, laying the foundations of modern Iranian national identity. Founded through Shāh Ismā'īl's charismatic leadership, the state transformed Isfahan into one of the world's most magnificent capitals under Shāh Abbās. While Ottoman-Safavid rivalry permanently shaped the Sunni-Shī'ī axis of the Middle East, Isfahan's Naqsh-e Jahān Square was built as one of Islamic architecture's most spectacular plazas. The Safavids also institutionalized the Iranian carpet tradition, miniature painting, and Shī'ī theology. The dynasty, which collapsed when the Afghan Ghilzai invasion took Isfahan, permanently determined Iran's political structure.

The 'Annāzids were a Kurdish principality ruling in a strategic position near the Iraqi border along the Hulwan-Dinavar axis. Reaching their peak under Abū al-Fatḥ Muḥammad's leadership, this dynasty played a role in regional balances during the Būyid-Seljuq transition period.

raw.capital: Firrim (1. kol); Sârî (2. kol); Amul (3. kol)
raw.narrative_en: The Bāwandid Ispahbadhs ruled in Tabaristan for nearly seven centuries, forming one of the Islamic world's longest-lasting dynasties alongside the Shirvanshahs. Governing in three separate branches, the Bāwandids successfully carried the Sasanian Ispahbadh tradition into the Islamic era.

The Dābūyids were a dynasty continuing the Iranian Ispahbadh tradition from the Sasanian era in the mountainous Tabaristan region. As one of the last Iranian principalities resisting Islam, they ended with the Abbasid army's conquest of the region.

The Hāshimids of Derbent governed Bāb al-Abwāb ('Gate of Gates'), the most strategic pass in the Caucasus, for two centuries, serving as a critical frontier fortress in Islam's northward expansion. Transitioning from Abbasid border governorship to a local dynasty, they defended the Islamic frontier against Khazar and Caucasian peoples.

The Justānids were a local Iranian dynasty governing the late-Islamizing communities in the mountainous Daylam region on the southern shore of the Caspian Sea. Facilitating Islam's penetration into this region, the Justānids can be considered precursors to the Būyids who emerged from the same geography.

The Kākūyids were a dynasty of Daylamī origin that rose in Isfahan and Hamadan as the Būyids weakened. Under 'Alā' al-Dawla's leadership, resisting both Ghaznavid Mahmud and Seljuq Tughril Beg, the Kākūyids continued as a small principality in Yazd after being expelled from Isfahan.

raw.regions_all: Tebriz; Sultaniye; İsfahan; Bağdat; Azerbaycan; Anadolu (tâbi); Gürcistan; Horasan
raw.successor: Celâyirliler; Muzafferîler; Kurtlar; çeşitli yerel hanedanlar
raw.narrative_en: The Il-Khanids were a Mongol state centered on Iran-Iraq-Azerbaijan founded by Hülegü. Having ended the Abbasid caliphate by destroying Baghdad, the Il-Khanids underwent transformation with Ghāzān Khān's conversion to Islam. Under Öljeitü, they left magnificent architectural works in Sultaniyya, creating the most brilliant example of the Mongol-Islamic synthesis.

names.ja: ジョージアパラリンピック委員会
